data.title: F5: EMEA Regulatory Tailwinds, But High Stock-Based Comp Is Worth Considering
data.description:
F5, Inc. (FFIV) reported a strong quarter this week, and the stock has responded accordingly, climbing 7%. At first glance, it probably looks like a straightforward beat-and-raise story, and in many ways it is. But I think there are a few things worth digging into beneath the surface, particularly around what the AI revenue numbers actually mean, whether the European regulatory tailwind has legs, and a valuation question that does not get enough attention in the coverage I have read. I am rating FFIV a ‘hold.’ The business is in good shape, but I think the stock is pricing in a lot of the good news already, and once you adjust for stock-based compensation, the multiple is less attractive than it appears.

A Look At The Latest Quarter

F5 reported a strong quarter for Q2’26 with revenues of $812 million (a beat of $29 million), up 11% compared to last year with product revenue advancing 22% to $411 million on the back of systems revenue up 26% to $226 million. Software revenue clocked in at $184 million, up 17%, of which subscription-based software made up 90% and grew 20%. Services revenue was up slightly by 2% to $401 million, while recurring revenue reached 70% of the total.

When looking at what led to the results this quarter, F5 had pretty strong growth across hybrid multicloud refreshes and expansions, with now 7 consecutive quarters of double-digit product growth underscoring platform momentum. Some of the key highlights included approximately $50 million in direct AI use-case sales in the first half, more than doubling year over year, as the company approached 100 customers deploying F5 solutions for AI data delivery, runtime security, and factory load balancing.

International strength stood out with EMEA revenue up 22% and APAC up 19%, which was helped by digital sovereignty initiatives and competitive displacements, and platform consolidations in verticals like healthcare and energy drove higher-capacity systems wins and footprint expansion during refresh cycles.

Looking at the company’s profitability, gross margins stood at 83.7%, up 60 bps compared to last year. Operating margins were 190 bps higher at 33.8%. This growth was driven by a surge in high-value product sales, particularly in AI-ready hardware and software subscriptions. Again, since 90% of their software revenue now comes from recurring subscriptions, the software means that once it’s built, this directly boosts their gross and operating margins. With the strong operating leverage, EPS of $3.90 was 14% higher compared to last year and was 44 cents above sell-side estimates.

As a software-adjacent business, F5 benefits from operating leverage, but when it comes to financial leverage, it has none. At quarter end, the company had a cash position of $1.46 billion. FCF conversion is also high, as on an operating cash flow of $366 million, FCF came out to $348 million. F5 generates cash upfront through deferred revenue, and we can see that play out on the liabilities section of the balance sheet, up 8.1% to $850 million.

On the earnings call, while management called out $100 million in share repurchase at an average price of $269 million, with FY’26 repurchases expected to be at least 50% of free cash flow, it’s worth pointing out that the share count only decreased by 2%, yet stock-based compensation as a percentage of revenue was 9.3% as a percentage of revenue. With $75.5 million of stock-based compensation during the quarter, almost three-quarters of those share repurchases aren’t actually creating value for shareholders. Rather, they are real expenses for the business and deserve to be deducted from EBITDA when analyzing valuation.

Despite a major breach last year where a China-nexus group using malware was found to have had long-term persistent access to F5's internal BIG-IP development environment, F5’s business has operated mostly without disruption.

One of the more underappreciated parts of the F5 thesis right now is the fact that European cybersecurity regulation is effectively forcing enterprise spending into exactly what F5 sells. NIS2, which became enforceable across EU member states in late 2024, mandates cybersecurity risk management, incident reporting, and operational resilience across a huge swath of "essential" and "important" industries: energy, healthcare, banking, transport, digital infrastructure, and more. DORA, the Digital Operational Resilience Act for financial services, has been fully in force since January 2025 with even more prescriptive requirements.

What this means practically is that enterprises (particularly in Europe) that deferred infrastructure refresh cycles are now under compliance deadlines. Hybrid multi-cloud architectures with consistent security controls across on-premises and cloud environments are no longer optional. They are mandated. F5's BIG-IP and Distributed Cloud Services platform is built for exactly this kind of deployment. CEO Locoh-Donou has been explicit about this tailwind on several of the last few earnings calls.

Germany only completed its NIS2 transposition in December 2025. Enforcement timelines are staggered across member states into 2027. The EU Cyber Resilience Act adds another layer with deadlines running into 2027-2028. To me, the 22% EMEA growth this quarter is not a one-off. More likely, it’s a reflection of a multi-year regulatory spend cycle that disproportionately benefits vendors with deep hybrid infrastructure footprints. And F5 is one of the clearest plays on that theme.

F5 Has A Strong Competitive Position, But Not Without Threats

But F5 isn’t the only name that’s playing in the sandbox on this trend. F5's BIG-IP platform is the enterprise standard for Layer 4-7 load balancing, SSL offload, WAF, and API security in hybrid environments. Enterprises with large, complex BIG-IP deployments don't switch lightly, and the F5’s CFO noted that competitive takeout rates have gone up "materially." My guess is that as hybrid multi-cloud complexity grows, platform consolidation is more attractive than managing a patchwork of point solutions. Companies like F5 that have breadth here likely have an advantage.

That said, I don't think the competitive picture is entirely comfortable. Citrix's NetScaler is still the most direct ADC rival, and Cloud Software Group has been pushing it hard into hybrid architectures. In the cloud-native space, Cloudflare (NET) is the competitor I watch most closely. It bundles content delivery, web application security, DDoS protection, and bot filtering into a single platform at a price point that is hard to argue with. The risk is not that Cloudflare rips out a Fortune 500 company's decade-old F5 deployment. It probably won't. But it doesn't have to in order to quietly win the next generation of enterprise customers before they ever become F5 customers.

The big hyperscalers are a slower-moving threat but still real. They’ve mostly continued to commoditize basic load balancing, pushing F5 further up the value stack. So far this has worked out fine for F5, but it is a dynamic worth monitoring.

What To Expect In Q3 And Beyond

F5 raised its FY’26 revenue growth outlook to 7-8% from the prior 5-6% and guided Q3’26 revenue to a range of $820-840 million, implying roughly 6.5% growth at the midpoint. In my view, this upward revision reflects the strong close rates and pipeline momentum management highlighted, particularly in hybrid multicloud refreshes. For the full year they continue to expect mid-single-digit software growth, double-digit systems growth, and low single-digit services growth, with the gross and operating margin ranges left unchanged at 82.5-83.5% and 34-35%, respectively. I believe the guidance strikes a balanced tone given the visibility they now have into the second half.

For Q3, the company flagged that higher component costs (primarily memory) will cause a sequential step-down in gross margin that’ll last into Q4, though they still see full-year margins holding within the guided band. To me this is a near-term dynamic worth watching, especially as memory pricing remains elevated through much of FY’27, but management seems to be somewhat confident that supply visibility remains solid for the current build plans.

Longer term, management noted that they expect “even stronger software growth next year than this year.” That tells me the current mid-single-digit pace is more of a base-effect story than a demand slowdown. On the systems side, they “do expect there to be a growth opportunity for the systems business” into next year, which I interpret as confirmation that the refresh-plus-expansion dynamic is not a one-year phenomenon. Given these factors and the strong momentum with 8 out of 8 beats in the last few quarters, I like the setup for the balance of the year (source: Bloomberg).

Valuation Isn't Cheap When Adjusting SBC

On valuation, F5 looks reasonable at first glance. On a forward P/E basis, the company trades at 19.3x earnings, which is a below-market multiple for a business that the sell-side has modeled at 17% EPS growth to the end of the decade (source: Bloomberg). But I think when looking at the valuation, it’s important to keep in mind some of the add-backs being made to EBITDA.

On EBITDA, analysts are expecting $946 million in EBITDA, which is consistent with the guidance assumptions from management. However, when looking at the company’s stock-based compensation, it’s increased quite materially in the last few years. According to Bloomberg, stock-based comp has increased from $162.9 million in FY’19 to $231.5 million in FY'25. At the same time, sales per employee have only increased from $421.1k to $469.5k. When deducting the company’s $231.5 million of stock-based comp from EBITDA (since I view this to be a real expense, despite it being non-cash), the true valuation is likely closer to 24.8x EV/EBITDA. To get there, I’m using a $17.7 billion enterprise value against $946 million in EBITDA less $232 million of SBC. So while it’s worth noting that forward EBITDA in FY’27 is materially higher at $1.24 billion, our same EV/(EBITDA less SBC) is still 17.6x, which is high relative to peers.

Bottom Line

F5’s hybrid multi-cloud thesis is real, and the EMEA regulatory tailwind is not going away anytime soon. But at roughly 24.8x EV/EBITDA once you strip out stock-based compensation, I don’t think the stock is cheap enough to get excited about right now. I want to see software growth start to inflect more meaningfully and hear about what management says at the May 28 Investor Day before getting more constructive. For now, I’d be more comfortable sitting on the sidelines.

data.title: SoFi Technologies' Plunge Is A Gift
data.description:
April 29th was a rough day for shareholders of SoFi Technologies, Inc. (SOFI). The stock dropped about 13.6% in afternoon trading after management announced financial results covering the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Objectively speaking, it was actually a good time for the company. Revenue, profits, and cash flows rose nicely. The firm continues to see an increase in its book value per share and tangible book value per share on a year-over-year basis. And guidance points toward additional significant growth throughout this year.

The problem, however, was the fact that the market decided to punish the company for falling short of analysts’ expectations when it came to both top line and bottom line performance. But honestly, I view this as a good opportunity for those who are bullish about the business. Depending on which metric you use, the stock is quite cheap. And when you factor in its continued expansion, it makes sense to be bullish on it right now. That's why I have no problem reaffirming it as a Buy candidate after previously upgrading it to that in March of this year.

A great time meets pessimism

By almost every measure you could look at, the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year was a good time for shareholders of SoFi Technologies. Management reported revenue of $1.10 billion. That happened to be 42.6% above the $771.8 million that the business reported a year earlier. And yet, the market seemed unhappy since revenue did fall short of what analysts anticipated to the tune of about $30 million. This overall expansion that the company saw was thanks to a continued rise in the number of members and products that it has. The company ended 2025 with 13.65 million members. But that number popped to 14.71 million by the first quarter of this year. As the number of members increases, the number of products, which would be the number of features that they utilize that generate revenue for the company, increases as well. That figure increased from 20.17 million at the end of last year to 22.16 million at the same time this year.

In my view, SoFi Technologies has developed into one of the most impressive growth engines out there, especially when it comes to the financial sector. Management continues to innovate, which draws in new customers. That generates more revenue and enables the launch of even more features. As the platform becomes larger and meets more of the needs of its users, it also becomes stickier, which means that users are more likely to continue using it in the future. As an example, during the final quarter of 2025, the firm launched SoFi Crypto, making it the first nationally chartered bank to launch crypto trading for consumers. That same quarter, it launched other features as well, including SoFi Pay and SoFi USD. The former of these helps to facilitate payment transfers utilizing blockchain technology. Users are able to send money to more than 30 countries quickly and at a low cost. The latter of these made the company the first nationally chartered bank to issue its own stablecoin on a public, permissionless blockchain.

But that's not all. During the first quarter of this year, the company also launched its Big Business Banking offering. Through this, the company will provide Fiat and crypto banking to businesses on a single regulated platform with business deposit accounts factored in and the ability to facilitate payments all hours of the day, every day of the week. What's also exciting is that the company continues to expand existing offerings as well. Throughout this year, it is expanding SoFi Coach, which will utilize AI in order to provide insights across all areas of financial activity for users. It will eventually answer questions, provide investment and lending options, help set and track goals, and more. Plus, the company is relaunching SoFi Technology Solutions, which will focus on enterprise clients that need a wide range of services such as processing, banking core ledgers, a payment hub, risk and fraud services, and more.

As revenue expanded, profitability improved also. Earnings per share went from $0.06 to $0.12, while adjusted earnings per share did the same. This translated to a rise in net profits and adjusted net profits from $71.5 million to $167.1 million. However, earnings per share and adjusted earnings per share did end up coming in $0.02 below what analysts had hoped for. So that certainly helped to facilitate the downside for the stock. We did see, it is worth noting, a rise in EBITDA as well. It rose from $210.3 million to $339.9 million. And as profitability for the company improved, both book value and tangible book value increased. The former rose from $6.05 per share last year to $8.44 per share this year. And the latter increased from $4.58 per share to $7.21 per share.

Thanks to continued innovation, management anticipates further expansion throughout 2026. They believe that revenue for the year will come in at around $4.655 billion. That would represent an increase of 28.8% year over year. They also expect EBITDA to expand from $1.05 billion to $1.6 billion, while adjusted net income should jump from $482.7 million to $825 million. As time progresses, we should expect even further expansion. This year, the adjusted earnings per share figure should be around $0.60, which is up substantially from the $0.39 reported for last year. But by 2028, as the company benefits from continued innovation and certain growth opportunities like the refinancing of almost $1 trillion of prime US credit card debt and personal loans, not to mention refinancing opportunities when it comes to student loans, the firm believes that earnings per share will expand to between $1.02 and $1.12.

Probably the best way to value the company would be to use the price-to-tangible-book approach. In the chart below, you can see how the institution stacks up against five other similar firms using this metric. Only one of these five companies is cheaper than it is. Of course, there are other ways to value the company, some of which paint it in a slightly less favorable light. The same chart shows what the company is valued at on an enterprise value-to-revenue basis and on a market capitalization-to-revenue basis. When it comes to the enterprise value approach, three of the five companies are cheaper than it is. And honestly, the stock is really only expensive when we use the market capitalization approach. In that respect, it is quite pricey. However, I still maintain that the price-to-tangible-book approach is the most sensible given the nature of the company.

Perhaps the biggest risk factor to the company is broader economic weakening. As I have detailed in other articles, the economy is in rough shape right now. And honestly, that picture will probably continue to deteriorate moving forward. But the good news is that what data is available shows fairly little in the way of risk for the company. Perhaps the biggest risk area would be personal loans. And as the image below illustrates, only 0.47% of these were 90 or more days delinquent. That actually is almost even with the 0.46% reported a year earlier and is below the 0.50% that I typically like to see. But if economic conditions do worsen as I expect they will, there could be some increase here. Hopefully, it would also mean that interest rates are declining, which could help to offset some of this weakness on a borrower-by-borrower basis.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's available right now, I firmly believe that the market is getting this one wrong. Based on valuation and growth opportunities, SoFi Technologies makes for a compelling prospect. Yes, the recent downside is disappointing. It's also unfortunate that management fell short of expectations when it came to revenue and profits. But in the grand scheme of things, I would view this as more of a bump in the road than an indicator of long-term viability. This does not mean that there aren't certain risks for investors to be aware of. Most notably, exposure to personal loans could prove to be a pain point for the company. But so far, that picture is looking solid.

data.title: ASML: The Question Is Whether Its Monopoly Can Outrun Its Premium Valuation
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered ASML (ASML) was in the summer of 2021, with the stock having appreciated 113% since, while the S&P 500 index is up by 69.5%. I’ve traded the stock a couple of times since then, as it has a tendency to trade in the range of $600-$1200 per share, but it has left that range behind it, as it’s currently at roughly $1450 per share.

I’ll come back to the competitive position of the company in detail later, but it really hasn’t changed much since the last time I covered the company. ASML remains the undisputed leader of extreme ultraviolet lithography (EUV) technology, commanding 100% global market share. Full stop. The older generation, deep ultraviolet lithography (DUV), comes with a little bit of competition as ASML commands a dominant position with roughly 90% global market share. As I’ll discuss later, this company comes with a durable moat that most can only dream of.

Best guesses are that replicating ASML’s leadership in EUV would take 10-20 years, cost in the billions of dollars, and require a coordinated supplier ecosystem. It’s uncertain that even the existing competitors will be able to close the technology gap in this decade, so imagining a completely new entrant appears unlikely.

Effectively, ASML is a monopoly in the global semiconductor stack, similar to TSMC (TSM) and Nvidia (NVDA), who also command close to monopoly-like positions across foundries and GPUs. Each is not replaceable in the short term, capacity constrained, and systemically important.

This is also why, similar to the others, ASML secures exceptional ROIC levels. I never shy away from discussing my preference for ROIC as the most important financial metric; in my perspective, it’s the most important metric for gauging long-term potential for a company. 15-20% ROIC levels are strong in a global perspective, but consistently securing 25% plus, as is the case for ASML, is exceptional and speaks to its moat and also its long-term ability to drive returns and grow the business. What’s very important to note here is that ASML has sustained such levels through cycles, which is what separates durable moats from temporary spikes in performance. Being a skilled capital allocator is what can drive long-term value.

ASML really is in a unique marketplace position, but this is also cause for concern and risk, as the superpowers of the day both vie for the lead in access to products made from ASML machinery, 3nm and 5nm chips.

Investment Thesis

ASML is a “toll bridge” monopoly, uniquely anchored by its cornered resource, exclusive control over EUV lithography. This structural advantage creates a moat that would take competitors decades to replicate. With 100% share in EUV and a dominant position in DUV, ASML is the sole enabler of the sub-5nm chips driving the AI revolution. Financially, the company is a powerhouse, maintaining ROIC levels above 25% and a consistent beat and raise cadence.

However, the current valuation warrants a disciplined approach. With the forward P/E trading near the top of its historical range of 38-40x, the margin of safety is thin. While the fundamental drivers justify a premium valuation, the stock is currently priced for near-perfection, or very strong industry outlooks at the very least. I believe ASML is a core long-term holding and industry winner, but the optimal strategy at these levels is a tranche-based entry to mitigate the risk of multiple normalizations or general market volatility.

State Of The Business & Why ASML Is A Superior Business Model

As mentioned, it’s been a couple of years since I last covered ASML, so here comes a bit about company development and outlook.

The last time I covered the company can be read here. The semiconductor sector was forecasted to grow at annual compounded growth rates of 8.6% through 2028, which translated to expectations for FY2025 ASML revenue at $32.3 billion. Actual FY2025 revenue came in at $38.4 billion, meaning consensus expectations were drastically undershooting actual performance. The reason? Out of the last seventeen quarterly reports, EPS was beaten on sixteen occasions and revenue on fourteen. Outstanding.

The reason is the semiconductor space, despite its already marvelous growth outlook, has performed even better than anticipated as a whole. ASML conducted an investor day at the end of 2024, where they shared an updated outlook for the industry.

The semiconductor ecosystem has considerable means to drive innovation, as the ecosystem covering equipment manufacturers, semi designers, hardware providers, and software and service providers chiseled out a combined $865 billion in EBIT during 2023. ASML expects the entire ecosystem to drive more than $1 trillion in revenue by the end of the decade, assumptions that I would conclude carry more upside than downside risk as AI continues to beat consensus expectations.

Given the application use and that companies across the sector expect continued semiconductor acceleration, as well as increased focus on high-performance computing, technology leaders stand to benefit as they potentially can widen the gap to laggards.

Hamilton Helmer, former Bain & Company consultant, later Ph.D. in economics from Yale and teacher at Stanford, has developed his seven powers framework that looks at where companies have durable competitive advantages, as to not rely merely on strategy. In other words, some companies are in a position where they’re incredibly difficult to compete with. I highly recommend reading up on his framework, and I’ve briefly highlighted it here in the case of ASML with a weak to strong rating.

Scale Economics: Strong

Large, fixed R&D and supplier ecosystem costs spread over a small number of extremely high-value systems. Competition can’t match cost efficiency without similar global volume and installed base, with service revenue further reinforcing scale advantages.

Network Economics: Moderate

Not a classic user network, but a deep ecosystem network with customers (the chipmaker) and suppliers (machinery parts, software). Process integration improved with shared learnings across customers and the inherent feedback loop. Benefits exist but don't follow a more classic network setup.

Counter positioning: Strong

This is often the least intuitive of the powers, so let’s take the example of Blockbuster vs. Netflix first. Streaming was the future, but eliminating late fees would destroy Blockbuster’s profits and business model, so they delayed their move into this segment, meaning the window for doing so closed permanently. Similarly, the incumbent competitors in DUV couldn’t respond to ASML’s push into EUV easily, as it would require investing billions, cannibalizing profitable DUV systems, rebuilding supply chains, and accepting negative returns for years. This isn’t a technology decision; it’s a balance sheet decision, and one they didn’t make, meaning they were put well behind in the race. They found themselves in a strategic trap where EUV appeared to be an option with high risk, low short-term returns, and representing a threat to its existing profile. As such, the counter-positioning for ASML is strong.

Counter-positioning as a power often closes over time, as what it does is buy you time, which in this industry also means time to leap to the next technological step. I’ve highlighted again and again in articles for semiconductor players that this is an industry relying entirely on process knowledge. No one can enter the semiconductor stack today and immediately be able to manufacture 3nm chips or EUV machinery. No, you need to build the process knowledge and capability in less complex structures first, meaning the path to enter the cutting-edge technology can be extremely long, which is also why we’ve seen the number of semiconductor companies across the stack dwindle over time, as some never managed to make the transition to a later technology, having to opt out of the market.

Switching cost: Very Strong

Lithography tools are deeply embedded in fab processes, meaning that switching vendors requires redesigning nodes and requalification. Downtime risk and yield loss make substitution economically prohibitive, and the installed base lock-in can last decades. There is a very big process risk associated with switching your installed base, and in a high-margin marketplace you don’t want to run that risk, and you want to make use of the best possible equipment that can secure a high yield in your own process for as long as possible. This isn’t a matter of preference between the taste of Coke vs. Pepsi; this is about high-margin output components that rely on the best possible equipment, so switching costs are very high both from a financial and risk perspective.

Branding: Weak to Moderate

The ASML brand signals reliability and technological leadership in a mission-critical industry. However, purchasing decisions would be driven primarily by performance and capability, not brand perception. As such, the brand supports trust but isn’t the core moat.

Cornered Resource: Very Strong

This is arguably the core power behind ASML today. The definition is that a company has exclusive control over a valuable asset that competition can’t access on similar terms. The key word here is, exclusive, so not just good or rare. Winning in this space means superior economic returns, so this particular power pins the notion of long-term superior returns for ASML. Please reference my comments on ROIC levels.

For ASML, the cornered resource isn’t a single patent, as would be the case for a pharma company, for instance. It’s a system of interdependent resources.

For ASML, the cornered resource solidity is about EUV optics capabilities, the EUV supplier ecosystem, accumulated process knowledge, and its installed base and service. These aspects drive a specialized setup that is non-transferable and path-dependent. In other words, competitors can’t buy it; they must build it, and it took decades and massive capital plus a coordinated ecosystem development to get there.

This power is durable when the resource is scarce, complex, cumulative, and embedded in a system, and ASML checks all four. This is also why we can observe a high gross margin, high ROIC, and limited price competition due to limited alternatives.

Process power: Strong

Highly optimized engineering, manufacturing, and service processes built over decades. Continuous improvement reduces defects and increases system uptime in extremely complex systems, with operational excellence compounding over time.

You could try and apply this framework to businesses across different industries. I have, and rarely do I see such a strong position across all as we can with some of the very unique semiconductor companies out there, particularly those where they effectively operate in a monopoly-like setup for either their full or parts of their offerings.

These aspects should also carry over into the valuation discussion, as you arguably should be willing to pay a premium for a company in a very entrenched and protected position that can drive high margins consistently.

Another Quarter Of Across-the-Board Financial Strength

In recent earnings releases, ASML has had a wonderful tendency of delivering solid beats, and Q1-2026 was no different. Here follows a brief summary of some of the financial highlights

  • Revenue rose YoY by 13% to €8.8 billion (€110 million beat)
  • GAAP EPS of €7.15 (€0.54 beat)
  • Gross margin of 53%, a 1 percentage point improvement (exceeded guidance)
  • Operating margin of 36%, a 1 percentage point improvement
  • FY2026 guidance increase to €36-€40 billion (up from €34-€39 billion)
  • Plans to declare a total dividend of €7.5, a 17% increase.
  • €8.4 billion in cash and equivalents and short-term investments
  • Inventory levels remained stable despite a 13% expansion in topline
  • Long-term debt of €2.7 billion

This is a company in stellar financial shape that beat on all possible parameters that were issued guidance for.

The profit & loss performance that secured better-than-expected margins was driven by a strong mix in the installed base segment where customers pay for upgrades and services. There is an insatiable appetite for AI infrastructure, which caused the FY2026 revenue upwards adjustments, so this is still the place to be in the semiconductor stack.

  • Out of €8.8 billion in revenue, €4.1 billion came from EUV, and €2.5 billion came from installed base management.

The narrative remains dominated by EUV, as can be seen above, and the CEO, Christophe Fouquet, noted during the earnings call that ASML is racing to ensure it won’t be the bottleneck in the global semiconductor value chain as they are fully booked. Essentially, the company is already trying to map how to deliver 80 EUV systems for 2027 while aiming to deliver 60 EUV systems this year.

Somewhat surprisingly, the old DUV business also indicated resilience despite concerns about a decline due to trade tension with China. On a higher level, however, the overall share going to China did drop to 19%, which is showing a declining trend but in line with management’s guidance of a 20% target.

Geographical demand remains focused primarily in South Korea, Taiwan, and China, making up 80-90% of revenue in any given quarter.

Only hair in the soup, and a possible reason why the stock reacted negatively on the earnings release, was that Q2-2026 revenue guidance was a bit softer than anticipated, which came in at €8.4-€9.0 billion, with a consensus of €9.1 billion.

Looking beyond the guidance for FY2026, the investment community has consistently elevated expectations of what ASML would be capable of delivering both short- and long-term towards the end of this decade.

Valuation

Here comes a key point about investing in the semiconductor space. In recent years, and still today, there is a high conviction in the market around infrastructure companies, whereas there is low conviction concerning software companies that might benefit or end up having their business model destroyed by the rapidly evolving AI space. We see multiple examples of this; take a company such as ServiceNow (NOW) that, despite beating expectations recently, was sent down almost 17% upon releasing its quarterly results, as the lingering risk around AI outcomes keeps the stock suppressed despite already having traded down substantially and showing a solid financial performance.

Similarly, infrastructure plays such as ASML, NVDA, etc., all keep seeing expanding margins over the recent years, not solely driven by a positive consensus outlook but due to the fact that a high conviction results in money inflows as opposed to funds flowing out. It’s difficult these days to find an infrastructure play that doesn’t carry a strong factor grading when it comes to momentum.

However, there probably comes a “Cisco” moment, where these valuation levels will have to normalize. Some have expected that moment to unfold in recent years, but now it seems unlikely to transpire in 2026, so the positive momentum appears to continue.

Just to stick with momentum for a moment. ASML, Nvidia, TSM, and Lam Research (LRCX) all carry momentum grades of B or better, meaning they are beating the sector mean performance across different momentum metrics. Whereas a company such as UiPath (PATH) or ServiceNow both have momentum grades of D-, which indicates negative and weak momentum.

I already discussed the seven powers in the case of ASML, which could support an acceptance for a premium valuation, so let’s take a closer look.

The first observation is that ASML is trading at the top end of its range in recent years, at what I would consider a premium valuation despite its solid growth. As mentioned, the company has managed to beat consensus expectations consistently in recent years to the point where it has compounded substantially, beating the, at the time, forward expectations with billions of euros. If this continues, the company could continue to make a fool of forward expectations. Management just upped the guidance for FY2026, and the investor consensus has upped FY2027 revenue expectations by 22% over the past six months alone. There is a lot of bullish sentiment around these companies, and while I have a hard time imagining that ASML should continue trading at close to a forward price-earnings ratio of forty many years from now, the logical arguments are in place for allowing that multiple at the current point in time.

It’s hard to argue we can focus exclusively on the past when trying to predict the future, especially in a situation where the potential AI boom didn’t transpire five years ago, making observations void or unreasonable to compare.

In my opinion, buying ASML at today's price comes across as fair, but it doesn’t come with a margin of safety, and it’s also difficult for me to imagine there’ll be such an opportunity just around the corner outside a broader market pullback.

I’m heavily exposed with semiconductor stocks making up more than 20% of my portfolio, so I’ll try and let time help me before I re-enter the stock, but if I wasn’t heavily exposed already, I would feel comfortable initiating a position here, as it appears to me the stock can still deliver returns on par with the market, but I would build my position in tranches as to limit the impact of broader market volatility.

Risks To My Thesis

While ASML’s monopoly status is undisputed, the following four factors could serve as vulnerabilities to what I’ve laid out.

First is what I would label adaptation friction, which refers to the transition to the next generation of lithography, High-NA EUV. This is proving more expensive and complex than what was the case with adjusting to Low-NA EUV. Notably, TSM has signaled a delay in adopting these very expensive systems while opting to stick with Low-NA EUV systems as the core of its setup. If other major foundries follow TSM’s lead to save on Capex, ASML could face a revenue gap, causing downward pressure on the very positive consensus outlook.

Secondly, to continue in the line of Capex, there is a very high concentration risk for ASML. ASML’s recent guidance hikes are heavily reliant on a continued, massive expansion in AI infrastructure spending. While we all know the demand picture of the Mag7 companies, more traditional industries such as automotive and industrial seem less adamant on a continued arms race, which could make the current AI super-cycle into a more moderate replacement cycle. With a premium valuation, ASML would be vulnerable to a significant multiple re-rating.

Third, there is the point of geopolitical tension, where the tech cold war between the U.S. and China could continue tightening export opportunities. We’ve seen this with the MATCH ACT, which doesn’t target just the EUV but also the DUV system as well as the highly profitable service revenue from Chinese customers. With 20% revenue exposure to China, a partial or system-related ban could prove to be a real wrench in the machinery of ASML.

Lastly, even the best monopoly-like company out there faces competition or companies that are trying to repeal the existing marketplace dynamic. This subsection of the semiconductor industry is no different, and existing and emergent competition can be broken into different themes. There is the concept of alternative lithography, which is commercialized by Canon in the form of its nanoimprint lithography, which bypasses ASML’s complex optical systems entirely by stamping patterns onto wafers using a mold rather than light. The technology is currently limited to memory chips, but Canon claims the capability of manufacturing down to 5nm chips and below with more power-efficient solutions compared to ASML EUV offerings. This could prove to be a viable alternative as technology advances. The other aspect of competition is related to the idea of a “good enough” competitor. While EUV remains undisputed, the DUV cash cow segments face more competitive pressure from both the Japanese Nikon as well as the Chinese-based Shanghai Micro Electronics Equipment, especially for larger nanometer chips. ASML retains a strong free cash flow turnout from DUV, which it uses to fund high-NA lithography, so erosion here can effectively hurt the company’s strategic position substantially.

Conclusion

ASML remains one of the most systemically important companies in the semiconductor stack, leveraging scale economics and high switching costs to maintain its lead. The Q1 2026 results and the 22% upward revision for FY2027 revenue expectations underscore a business that continues to outpace even the most bullish consensus. It has successfully navigated geopolitical headwinds with reference to US-China relations while capitalizing on the insatiable demand for AI infrastructure.

From a valuation perspective, the data shows ASML is trading at a significant premium compared to its 5Y and 10Y mean levels. While the “AI boom” makes historical comparisons difficult, the lack of a valuation cushion suggests that any deviation from management’s guidance could lead to volatility. In summary, ASML is a peerless business at a fair, albeit steep, price. For investors not already overexposed to the sector, initiating a position in steps allows for participation in the AI supercycle while protecting against the inevitable “Cisco moment” of market-wide valuation resets.

data.title: Upstart's Record-Breaking New Product Launch Strengthens My Bullish View
data.description:
As many of my close followers know, I have been bullish on Upstart Holdings, Inc. (UPST) for quite some time. In my previous analysis published in early March, I claimed that Upstart is doing the right thing by applying for a bank charter. UPST stock is up 20% since then, but these gains are insignificant compared to the 70%+ decline in Upstart stock in the past 5 years.

In this analysis, I will focus on Upstart's latest product, Cash Line. This was announced in February. In a nutshell, Cash Line is a revolving credit facility aimed at retail customers. On April 15, Upstart announced the official launch of Cash Line on X. A day later, Co-Founder and CTO Paul Gu (incoming CEO) confirmed that Cash Line was off to a great start, becoming the best-ever product launch in the history of the company based on usage.

Exhibit 1: Paul Gu's X post

After evaluating the Cash Line product and the market opportunity for Upstart, I am confident that this could turn out to be a high-margin business for the company. This strengthens my bullish stance on Upstart ahead of its upcoming earnings on May 5th.

Upstart Cash Line

Cash Line is a revolving line of credit product. This product launch marks a deviation from Upstart's previous focus on lump-sum loans such as personal loans, auto loans, and small business loans. Below are some of the important characteristics of the Cash Line product based on official company documents.

  • Approved users will receive a minimum credit limit of $200. Limits are capped at $5,000 per user.
  • Once approved, users can instantly transfer the approved amount to a bank at no cost. The goal, therefore, is to compete with established cash advance apps such as Dave.
  • Upstart will charge a flat $10 monthly fee for credit limits of up to $500. For any revolving facility draws that exceed $500, an APR of 5% to 36% will be applicable.

In addition to the likes of Dave, I believe Upstart Cash Line will eventually end up competing with BNPL players such as Klarna (KLAR) as well.

Cash Line's TAM Is Massive

I believe Cash Line can capture a share of 3 different lending sectors within the larger U.S. market.

  1. The payday loan market
  2. Bank overdraft market
  3. Earned Wage Access market.

I will briefly highlight the opportunity that I see for Cash Line within these markets.

The U.S. is one of the biggest payday loan markets in the world. This should not come as a surprise to any investor following U.S. markets closely given the strong appetite Americans have shown for credit products in the past few decades. If you want a confirmation of this, just have a quick look at Americans' love for credit cards. According to Capital One, more than 216 million U.S. adults had a credit card account as of 2025, which represents more than 80% of the total U.S. adult population. Compare this to less than 65% in many other developed countries and regions, including the UK, Australia, New Zealand, Germany, and Sweden.

Exhibit 2: Number of credit card accounts in the U.S.

As you can clearly see from the above data, American adults often use more than one credit card.

Coming back to payday loans, according to IBIS World, the check cashing and payday loan market was valued at $20.5 billion in 2024 on the back of a ~3% CAGR in the preceding 5-year period. Data from Market Research Future highlights that approximately 12 million Americans use these payday loan products annually. This makes the payday loan market a large subsegment of the U.S. finance sector.

The bank overdraft market is structurally larger. According to Financial Health Network, Americans spent ~$12 billion on overdraft and NSF fees in 2024 alone.

Exhibit 3: Bank overdraft fees paid by Americans in 2024

More granular data shows that 11% of all banking adults in the U.S. paid an overdraft fee in 2024. This highlights the sheer size of the overdraft market.

The EWA market is not small either. The key difference here is that this is more of a liquidity bridge market than an outright lending market. Some of the most popular DTC apps/platforms in this market include Dave and EarnIn. There are employer-sponsored platforms such as DailyPay operating in this market as well. I could not find EWA data for 2025, and the most reliable data goes back to 2022. Based on Consumer Finance Protection Bureau data, EWA platforms disbursed $22.8 billion to 7.2 million users in 2022.

Overall, I believe Upstart's Cash Line is well-positioned to capture a share of all these market segments in the long run. In the next segment, I will discuss the reasons behind this expectation.

Cash Line Will Bring A High-Margin, Recurring Revenue Stream To Upstart

One of the main reasons behind my strong growth expectations for Cash Line is the cost advantages offered by this product. As I noted earlier, Cash Line is available at a $10 monthly subscription fee for borrowings of less than $500. In the payday loan market, this translates to a very low fee given that established payday lenders charge anywhere between $10 and $20 for every $100 borrowed on their platforms. For instance, Advance America charges $15 per $100 borrowed for a 2-week period. The APRs, as you can imagine, are sky-high.

This is where the Upstart Cash Line product comes into play with a flat monthly subscription fee. For the 12 million Americans who use payday loan platforms annually, Cash Line offers a significant cost advantage over established legacy players. In plain terms, Upstart is undercutting these platforms big time. I believe these cost advantages will be one of the biggest drivers of Cash Line's growth in the long run.

Next, Cash Line offers a seamless process for borrowers to access cash after repaying since this is structured as a revolving credit facility. Payday loan platforms, on the other hand, require users to reapply all over again to get access to credit. I believe this structural product advantage will also be a driver of Cash Line's growth.

When it comes to capturing a share of the bank overdraft market, a recent regulatory tailwind will boost Cash Line's appeal. Back in December 2024, the CFPB approved a rule capping the overcharge fees a large bank can charge from a borrower at just $5. There were some provisions to hike this fee, but the new rules made these provisions difficult to attain. However, last September, this overdraft fee capping rule was overturned by Congress. I believe this leaves the door open for Upstart's Cash Line product to disrupt this market.

Capturing a share of the overdraft fee market will go beyond just boosting current period revenue, as this opens doors for Upstart to secure new deposits if its bank charter is approved. Cash Line will essentially act as a marketing tool to secure new deposits as borrowers will be drawn into the Upstart ecosystem.

Even if Cash Line captures just 1% of the payday market (around 120,000 users), we are looking at incremental revenue of $14.4 million. This represents the worst-case scenario, in my opinion. In the base-case scenario where Cash Line eventually captures a 5% share of the payday loan market (600,000 users), revenue will get a $72 million boost. For context, this represents 5%+ of the management's revenue guidance for 2026.

In the above estimates, I am only looking at subscription fee revenue. In reality, though, the company's net interest income should also get a boost, assuming its bank charter is approved.

Risks

As I often highlight in my articles, Upstart has a lot to prove as a fintech that can remain resilient during challenging economic conditions. This is what the company failed to do when the Fed turned hawkish the last time around. I'm sure many Upstart investors are still suffering from the losses incurred back then. There are good signs, but an eventual credit market crunch can still push Upstart into challenging times. This risk cannot be ignored.

I am also concerned about the capital maintenance requirements that are attached to bank charters. Return on equity is likely to be impacted when Upstart is forced to maintain CET1 ratios. Keeping an eye on Upstart stock's sensitivity to interest rates is also key if the bank charter is approved.

Takeaway

Upstart offers the best of both the financial services industry and the tech sector. I believe the company is still in the early stages of its growth story and that deeper expansion into auto and SME loans will boost the company's profitability in the long run. My bullish view on Upstart is strengthened by the strong start to Cash Line, Upstart's revolving credit product. I believe Cash Line will bring a stable, recurring revenue stream to Upstart while enabling the company to expand into a new market segment.

data.title: MTUM: Momentum Investing Is Poised To Thrive In 2026
data.description:
iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF (MTUM) has the potential to deliver more than 20% in price return for shareholders in 2026, in-line with its average return in the past three years. While MTUM frequently changes its portfolio holdings to capitalize on a momentum factor based on the market conditions, its current portfolio appears well-balanced to achieve solid risk-adjusted returns. In the trailing three and ten years, MTUM’s price return of 95% and 275% demonstrates the effectiveness of MTUM’s strategy over the long term. I maintain my buy rating on MTUM.

Momentum Investing Can Flourish for the Fourth Consecutive Year

Momentum investing is deemed as one of the most powerful strategies to earn market-beating returns, specifically during bull runs and secular growth trends. In the trailing three-year bull run, MTUM’s momentum strategy delivered an impressive capital appreciation of 95%. On an annual basis, its average price return was 25% in the past three years.

In 2026, iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF has the potential to deliver more than 20% in price return. My optimism in its share price upside is attributed to the strong stock market outlook. The key upside catalysts include a low interest rate environment compared to the previous years and the strongest earnings growth forecast in the last three years.

The S&P 500 is expected to generate 18.5% earnings growth in 2026, the fastest pace in the past three years. In particular, the technology sector is likely to drive the uptrend, with expectations for a 38% year-over-year increase. Last year, the technology sector’s earnings grew 25% from the previous year. Although CME data indicates a low probability of a rate cut in the coming months due to the impact of higher oil prices on inflation, I believe a change in the Federal Reserve chair can fuel a policy shift. President Trump has criticized Jerome Powell over his tight interest rate policy. He expects Kevin Warsh to reduce rates to boost economic growth.

Wall Street also anticipates an extension of the uptrend, with expectations for the S&P 500 to deliver a 15% to 17% increase in 2026. MTUM has the potential to beat the broader market index due to its exposure to stocks with a relatively higher price momentum. In fact, the S&P 500 uptrend and MTUM’s outperformance are already underway.

After a short, war-driven selloff and bottoming in late March, MTUM generated a blistering share price rally of 19%, beating the S&P 500 by 8%. I expect this trend to continue throughout 2026 with a few shortfalls. The current rally is driven by the stronger-than-expected first-quarter results and a ceasefire between the US-Iran. Oil prices are also expected to fall once both countries reach a final agreement.

MTUM Stock Selection Strategy

Before digging into iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF’s current portfolio, it is crucial to take a brief look at its stock selection strategy. The fund’s strategy indicates that it seeks to limit a risk factor when chasing high-momentum stocks. MTUM tracks the MSCI USA Momentum SR Variant Index by using a representative sampling technique. The index seeks to hold stakes in stocks based on their 6- to 12-month risk-adjusted price momentum.

According to the MSCI, to obtain a risk-adjusted price momentum metric, the index divides excess return over the risk-free rate (based on the 3-month Treasury Bill) by an annualized standard deviation. Moreover, to get to an average momentum score, the index standardizes risk-adjusted price momentum figures. The index selects 120 to 130 stocks with the highest momentum score. The index also seeks to cap each security weight at 5% to reduce a risk factor. The rebalance occurs on a quarterly basis to remove or decrease weight in lagging stocks while replacing them with other high-momentum stocks.

MTUM’s Current Portfolio Can Capitalize on Upside Momentum

iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF's portfolio is spread across all 11 sectors, with a greater concentration in the technology and industrials stocks, collectively accounting for nearly 55% of its portfolio weight. The technology sector is expected to deliver a whopping 45% earnings growth in the first quarter and 37% for the full year. On the back of solid earnings growth power, the sector begins to rebound sharply. Since bottoming in late March, the Vanguard Information Technology ETF (VGT) is up 22%.

From the technology sector, MTUM’s portfolio is concentrated mainly in the semiconductor industry, the hottest area in the tech sector due to the robust demand for AI-powered chips and equipments.

With a 16% weight in the industrials sector, the ETF seeks to capitalize on positive momentum in industrials stocks. Year-to-date, the S&P 500 industrials sector is up 10%. The sector is expected to deliver 15% earnings growth in 2026. In particular, MTUM is targeting the aerospace and defense industry due to the record-breaking demand and massive backlogs. General Electric Company (GE), RTX Corporation (RTX), Howmet Aerospace Inc. (HWM) and Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT) are its top stocks from the aerospace and defense industry.

On the negative side, the financials sector's momentum faded in the past few months due to softening financial growth and uncertainty over rate cuts. MTUM's portfolio concentration in the financials sector is around 11%. MTUM's stake in the communications sector is attributed to its 4.8% weight in Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL). Alphabet’s share price rallied nearly 120% in the past twelve months. At present, cyclical tailwinds are supporting an upward momentum in the energy, real estate, basic materials and utilities sectors.

MTUM Characteristics

iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF’s beta of 1.14 indicates its potential to generate market-beating returns. Moreover, the high-momentum ETFs often trade at premium valuations. MTUM’s price-to-earnings ratio of 35x is above the 27x of the S&P 500 index, and its price-to-book ratio of 6.2x is also high.

MTUM’s buy rating with a high score of 4.21 based on the Seeking Alpha quant model is in-line with my analysis. The fund’s price momentum earned an A grade. A higher momentum score signals that an uptrend is expected to continue. Its expense ratio of 0.15% is cheaper than the median of all ETFs of 0.50%. MTUM’s assets under management hover around $24 billion. In the last three months, its average shares trading volume was nearly 850K.

MTUM Versus SPMO

Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO) is the closest peer of iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF. SPMO is a top-performing ETF with a quant score of 4.36, while MTUM ranks at the second spot. In particular, SPMO’s strong price momentum lifted its quant rating. In the trailing three years, SPMO’s price return of 131% outshone MTUM’s 95%.

SPMO’s outperformance is attributed to its greater exposure to tech stocks and higher concentration in its top holdings. SPMO’s top 10 holdings represent almost half of its portfolio weight, up from MTUM’s 38%. SPMO caps each security weight at 9% compared to MTUM’s 5%.

In addition, SPMO selects stocks based on their 12-month volatility-adjusted price momentum. A volatility-adjusted approach generally involves selecting higher momentum stocks while avoiding high volatility scenarios. This approach seems more aggressive compared to a risk-adjusted momentum strategy. In a risk-adjusted momentum strategy, MTUM considers a broader risk by dividing excess return over the risk-free rate by an annualized standard deviation. This is also reflected in SPMO’s standard deviation of 17 compared to MTUM’s 15.

In my recent article, I provided a buy rating on SPMO due to its strong upside potential. I believe it could be a compelling investment vehicle for aggressive to extremely aggressive risk-tolerance investors. However, for the moderate to aggressive risk-tolerance investors, MTUM is a better choice to access risk-adjusted momentum investing.

Risk Factors

Momentum investing carries a higher risk factor than value, dividend, and quality strategies. Momentum ETFs are vulnerable to sudden price downtrends as the high beta stocks can fall at a faster pace than the broader market indices.

MTUM’s current portfolio is exposed to challenges in the tech and industrial sectors. The tech-driven selloffs can negatively impact its performance. It can also underperform during bear market trends. In 2022, MTUM’s share price declined 21%, slightly higher than the S&P 500’s decline of 20%. Its standard deviation of 15 is also up from the median of all ETFs of 11. The low risk-tolerance investors are suggested to consider low-volatility strategies, including value and dividend.

In Conclusion

Momentum investing is likely to thrive in 2026 as the S&P 500 and other broader market indices are expected to extend their bull run into the fourth consecutive year. The earnings growth trend and recent valuation correction support the uptrend.

Therefore, iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF also has the potential to deliver more than 20% in price return in 2026. Its current portfolio appears well-balanced to capitalize on the potential uptrend. In addition, the fund’s quarterly rebalancing strategy boosts its ability to increase or decrease exposure to outperforming and underperforming stocks. The fund’s liquidity is high and expense ratio is low.

data.title: TransUnion: Continued Organic Growth And A Smart Acquisition (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

TransUnion (TRU) reported its Q4 results this week, and the headline numbers were strong across all metrics. The company just closed arguably its most significant acquisition in years, which I believe should set it up for continued growth longer term. For a business operating in what is effectively a three-player oligopoly alongside Equifax (EFX) and Experian (EXPGF), TransUnion has increasingly pushed into higher-value analytics and fraud solutions that go beyond the traditional credit pull that most of us are probably familiar with when we think about one of these credit bureaus. At 14.0x forward earnings with a credible path to low-to-mid teens EPS growth, I think the stock is worth a closer look.

A look at the most recent results

TransUnion reported total revenue of $1.25 billion for Q1 2026, an increase of 14% or 11% on an organic constant currency basis compared to the prior-year period. That came in $41 million above the high end of guidance and $28 million ahead of consensus estimates, which is a meaningful beat relative to how the company had framed the quarter.

Looking at the results by business segment, U.S. markets grew 14%, with Financial Services leading the way, up 24.0%, or 14.0% excluding FICO mortgage royalties. Strength came through across lending verticals, pricing actions, and continued adoption of solutions like TruIQ and Trusted Call Solutions. Mortgage revenue surged 50%, with a brief February refinancing lift contributing, though the underlying pricing and new business wins did most of the work. Emerging Verticals including insurance and the public sector, climbed more than 6.3%, while the Consumer Interactive business was 1.3% higher compared to last year.

Internationally, results were flat on an organic constant currency basis, which was in line with guidance. By geography, Africa grew 10% with strength across banking, FinTech, and retail, while India declined 5%, which was slightly better than guided. Management noted on the earnings call that they are expecting a gradual consumer lending there to support mid-single-digit growth there in 2026. Latin America was essentially flat organically.

Looking at TransUnion's profitability this quarter, EBITDA clocked in at $437.9 million, 10.3% higher compared to last year. That was good for 35.2%, as shown below, but that was roughly a 100 bps contraction compared to last year. This was somewhat expected given it was largely a function of the FICO royalty mix, which carries no margin. On EPS, earnings per share came in at $1.18, 12% higher than last year and 7 cents ahead of consensus.

As for the balance sheet, TransUnion does have some leverage with $5.6 billion of debt against a cash position of $733 million. The reason for the step up in debt this quarter was a result of the Mexico acquisition (which I'll discuss shortly) and was funded through revolver draws and existing cash, which lifted leverage modestly to 2.8x on a net basis. On the conference call following the quarter, management noted that even though this did increase leverage, their priority is to get back towards the sub-2.5x target over time while still returning capital through buybacks.

As far as TransUnion's own credit rating is concerned, S&P has a Ba2 rating with a stable outlook, and Moody's has a BB+ rating, also with a stable outlook (source: Bloomberg). With a credit profile that's just below the cusp of investment grade, TransUnion has access to a relatively low cost of debt, which they've obviously used to fund their latest deal. At present, they're currently paying SOFR+1.75% on their revolver, and the company's Term Loan B (the largest piece in the capital structure) is currently at SOFR + 2.25% (source: Bloomberg).

Mexico acquisition is a smart deal at a fair price

I think the biggest item worth talking about here is TransUnion's recent closing of the TransUnion de México acquisition, which closed on March 2. Trans Union de México, despite bearing nearly the same name, was a completely separate company and had been owned by Mexico’s largest credit bureau, Buró de Crédito. Up until the deal, TransUnion had owned 26% of the business and had some influence on the board through board seats.

By increasing its ownership from a minority 26% to approximately 94%, TransUnion has effectively positioned itself as the dominant force in the Mexican financial services market. The consideration was approximately $662 million, funded through a combination of the revolving credit facility and cash on hand.

On the valuation paid, the acquisition was expected to generate around $145 million in revenue and $70 million in adjusted EBITDA, implying a purchase multiple of roughly 9.4x EV/EBITDA (source: Bloomberg). For context, TransUnion itself trades at around 13x EBITDA, so the deal was struck at a meaningful discount to where the acquirer trades, which I think is a reasonable entry point for a business with this kind of structural growth potential.

With TransUnion now steering the primary credit data engine in Mexico, the company looks like it can introduce sophisticated risk management tools and alternative data solutions to a region where nearly half the population remains unbanked or credit invisible. So that's the real long-term thesis here. Mexico is a large, underpenetrated credit market, and TransUnion's playbook of layering in trended data, fraud solutions, and analytics on top of a core bureau asset is exactly what it has done successfully in markets like the UK, Canada, and Africa. The company also plans to expand beyond traditional financial services into adjacencies like FinTech and insurance, which is where the incremental margin opportunity likely sits.

On the earnings call, management said that they expect the deal to be modestly accretive to adjusted EPS in year one. I think the accretion assumptions are conservative. Once TransUnion layers its global product suite into Buró de Crédito over the next two to three years, the revenue per data pull should expand meaningfully beyond what the standalone bureau was generating. The slight leverage bump from the acquisition is the main near-term consideration, but at 2.8x net leverage with strong free cash flow, I don't see it as a concern. Shown below, we can also see that the general direction of leverage has been lower and down from 3.8x in 2022.

Competition and where TransUnion stands

It's worth stepping back to appreciate the structural position TransUnion occupies. Globally, more than 50-60% of the credit bureau market is dominated by three players: Experian, Equifax, and TransUnion. The barriers to entry in this business are pretty high. Decades of proprietary consumer credit data, deep lender integrations, and a regulatory compliance infrastructure that takes years to build make it essentially impossible for a new entrant to replicate what the incumbents have. The competitive dynamic among the three is less about stealing each other's lunch and more about who can layer the most value-added analytics and decisional tools on top of the core credit data asset.

That framing matters for understanding where TransUnion is investing. As other Seeking Alpha analysts have pointed out, Equifax's differentiation has been in Workforce Solutions, the employment and income verification business. Experian has the largest international footprint and a strong consumer-facing franchise.

TransUnion has increasingly leaned into fraud, alternative data, and AI-enabled decisioning tools like TruIQ and is building out Trusted Call Solutions as a meaningful emerging vertical. In my view, this positioning is differentiated enough that the company isn't simply trading on the same macro drivers as Equifax and Experian, and the AI investment cycle the company has been talking about is starting to show up in customer adoption and data consumption volumes.

How achievable are management's targets?

Management maintained full-year guidance for 8-9% organic constant currency revenue growth and 9-11% adjusted diluted EPS growth. I think those targets are conservative for a few specific reasons worth unpacking.

Firstly, this is the third consecutive year of guiding to high single-digit organic growth, and the company has beaten the high end of its guidance in each of the last eight quarters. To me, that seems to reflect a management team that underpromises and overdelivers. I interpret the guide as a base case for what they can control with the more variable factors like mortgage volumes and emerging market improvement as upside. TransUnion's CEO reinforced this on the call, noting that with volumes stable, "we orient you towards the high end of our guidance". That, to me, is about as close to a 'wink-wink, nudge-nudge' as you're going to get.

Secondly, the Q1 beat itself was also broad-based rather than driven by one-time items. Stripping out the FICO mortgage royalty impact, underlying organic growth of 7% still came in above expectations. That tells me the core business momentum, the pricing actions, the TruIQ adoption, and the Trusted Call Solutions traction are not entirely dependent on a favorable rate environment to sustain themselves.

If U.S. financial services lending activity holds at current levels and the mortgage refi market gets even a modest additional tailwind from any rate movement, that becomes incremental upside. The company noted that every 10% increase in mortgage volume would add approximately $43 million to adjusted EBITDA, while a full recovery to 2019 mortgage levels would translate to approximately $240 million in additional EBITDA (currently doing $1.3 billion for the LTM period).

By geography, the UK, Canada, and Africa are all growing at solid rates, and the drag is concentrated in India and parts of Asia Pacific, where management expects a gradual recovery. I don't see the international segment as a concern, with management noting that the business is positioned to "pivot back toward growth" in India and return to sustained low double-digit growth over time. For investors with a longer term time horizon, I do think that the Mexico acquisition also gives the company a high-quality growth asset in one of the most attractive emerging markets for credit penetration globally. That should start adding more meaningfully to the international story as the integration matures.

What to expect for next quarter

For Q2 2026, management guided revenue to $1.271-1.283 billion, which represents 8-9% organic growth and implies EBITDA in the range of $439-445 million with EPS of $1.13-1.15. Currently, the sell-side consensus is at revenue of $1.265 billion, EBITDA of $442.5 million, and EPS of $1.16, which is essentially right in line with the guidance range (Source: Bloomberg).

Given that TransUnion has beaten EPS estimates in 8 of the last 8 quarters, I think the setup for Q2 is reasonably constructive. The guidance range itself looks like a floor rather than a ceiling if current volume trends hold. The one thing to watch is the FICO royalty contribution, which management expects to start anniversarying in the back half, which should make the reported growth rate look a bit cleaner on a year over year basis. Mexico will also start showing up in the reported numbers for a full quarter in Q2 for the first time, adding a small but incremental contribution. How integration goes in the early stages will be key for investors to watch.

Risks and valuation

I've got a 'strong buy' rating on TransUnion, but there are obviously risks to consider given the macro sensitivity, particularly in mortgages. Mortgage revenue surged 50% in Q1, largely on the back of a brief February refinancing lift. If rates move the wrong way and that tailwind reverses, the headline growth rate gets harder to sustain even if the underlying business is healthy. India and emerging market softness are something to watch too since the international segment needs to start contributing more meaningfully to the overall growth story as U.S. growth eventually normalizes.

At 14.0x earnings and 12.7x on FY 2027 numbers, TransUnion looks cheap relative to peers like Equifax. With peers trading at 19.5x earnings or 17.7x on a forward basis, the discount here is hard to ignore (Source: Bloomberg). It's also worth pointing out that this valuation is at a low point in terms of where the stock has traded in the last five years.

Bottom line

TransUnion's stock has been range bound in the last few years, but the valuation has gotten cheaper, which is why I'm upgrading my rating. With several back-to-back quarters of continued organic growth, this is a name I think has a moat in the credit bureau space. For me, the Mexico deal adds a high-quality growth asset at a sensible price, and the balance sheet, while modestly more leveraged post-close, is not a concern given the free cash flow profile. At 14x forward earnings with a track record of beating estimates and a clear path to low-to-mid teens EPS growth, I think TransUnion is worthy of a 'strong buy' rating.

data.title: The Fed Dissents Signal A Pivot Towards Rate Neutrality
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier today, the Federal Reserve met and decided to maintain the current Federal funds rate as it navigates the uncertainty in price stability following shocks to the global energy markets. While investors anxiously await the effects of global energy markets on broader consumer pricing, a subtle dissent in the Fed statement points to the Fed possibility reframing the neutral interest rate.

Interest Rate Outlook

Nobody expected the Federal Reserve to change rates in today’s meeting, but the markets are still a helpful tool in determining where investors believe short-term rates are heading. Since the January meeting, the Fed fund futures curve has gotten progressively flatter. Since the last Fed meeting, 2027 Fed fund rates have moved up by about 10 basis points.

As for the current state of interest rates, investors as late as January felt that the Federal Reserve would have cut one more time by now. But consistent inflation pressures and the conflict with Iran have had the markets settling at no change. It is hard to believe that a year ago the markets projected that rates today would be 50 basis points lower than where we ended up.

For the end of this year, the markets are not far off from their current levels in terms of where they expect short-term rates to be. In fact, the Iran conflict caused Fed fund futures to jump 60 basis points for the month of December. They have since come down by about 20 basis points and remain above 3.5%. This signals that the market is expecting a coin flip over whether there will be another rate cut in 2026.

Looking beyond 2026, the market seems to be skeptical as to when the Fed will cut rates. While the market and the Fed are close in where things will end up in 2026, the market stays between 25 and 50 basis points ahead of the Fed’s projections until nearly 2028, when Fed funds futures drop to match the Fed’s neutral interest rate.

…But Things May Be Changing…

Things may be changing for the Federal Reserve, and it’s not just in regard to the Chairman position. At today’s meeting, there were four Federal Reserve governors dissenting. One dissenter wanted lower interest rates. The other three dissented for a reason that seems innocuous but could end up being very meaningful.

Board members Hammack, Kashkari, and Logan dissented not because of the target rate for federal funds, but because they “did not support inclusion of an easing bias in the statement.” These three members wanted to send a clear message that the Fed’s next move may not be one to cut rates.

The dissent signals a desire of some on the Federal Reserve to move towards the current interest rate level being the neutral rate. It could permanently remove future rate cuts from the Fed’s agenda unless substantial risks to the labor market or deflation were to form in the economy. This signals a desire to hold versus tapping the brakes.

Conclusion

Because we are in the early stages of a price disruption, it does not make any sense for the Fed to commit to any course of action. During the Russia invasion of Ukraine, it took four months for the effects of the energy price spike to hit consumer prices. I’m expecting the same here and do not believe the Fed will commit to any course of action for most, if not all of 2026. The next meeting in June involves updated economic projections. Investors will need to watch closely to see if the Fed extends its pause further or permanently.

data.title: Luckin Coffee: Evidence Of Cannibalization As Comps Erode (Downgrade)
data.description:
We've landed in the middle of the Q1 earnings season, and with the stock market already sitting near all-time highs and investors nervously attuned to macro volatility, we're in for a potentially bumpy quarter. In my view, it's a good time for investors to reshuffle our portfolio holdings, especially as fundamental stories change.

Luckin Coffee Inc. (LKNCY), in particular, is a stock I've soured substantially on since the start of the year. The Chinese coffee chain, which is aiming to take on Starbucks (SBUX) as the world's default coffee brand with new locations outside of China, has jumped ~6% after posting Q1 results. I, however, only see ongoing risks in the business as comp sales fade and margins compress.

I last wrote a neutral article on Luckin in March, when the stock was trading slightly higher in the mid-$35s. After digesting the company's Q1 results and growth slowdown, however, I'm coming away more disappointed with the company's execution. Luckin is prioritizing "growth at all costs," but in my view it's sacrificing not just profitability, but actual per-store performance. I'm downgrading the stock to a Sell rating.

In my view, Luckin is now a grab basket of red flags. The core risks to me include:

  • Clear evidence of cannibalization as Luckin opens stores too quickly. Yes, it's true that Luckin has its sights set beyond China. And yet its pace of openings may be too dense in certain markets. The company is currently opening stores at a pace of 28 per day, or more than one per hour. At the same time, comparable sales are declining, which is a reflection of a still-choppy global macro and potential cannibalization of existing locations.
  • Increased reliance on delivery is taking a toll on Luckin's margin profile. Though Luckin is hyper-focused on expanding its store network of both company-owned and franchise locations, in reality a rising mix of Luckin's sales is actually from third-party delivery platforms, which is forcing Luckin to pay out platform and delivery fees. This is dragging down the company's operating margins.
  • Tremendous competition overseas. Luckin's presence in overseas markets, especially in the U.S., is still brand new. There's a large chance that foreign consumers may reject Luckin in favor of local incumbents (the company has a reputation for funky flavors that may be off-putting to many coffee drinkers). In addition, in markets like the U.S., fast food giants like McDonald's (MCD) are stepping up their drinks game, signaling that new markets may not be that easy to crack.
  • Sharp increase in marketing expenses. The company is under pressure to keep up its advertising and social media presence. Sales and marketing expenses are rising much more quickly than revenue, which, on top of higher delivery expenses, is also contributing to margin erosion.

In my view, it's now best to sell off your Luckin position and move to the sidelines. I expect a tough global macro will wear down comp sales trends at other coffee chains like Dutch Bros (BROS) that have not yet reported results; in my view, it's best to reduce our exposure to names that are subject to volatile consumer spending.

Q1 Recap

Let's now go through Luckin's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Luckin's revenue grew 35% y/y to RMB 12.0 billion, or roughly $1.74 billion. Notably, the company is benefiting from a larger contribution of franchise/partner sales, with franchise revenue up 45% y/y to RMB 3.02 billion, roughly a quarter of the company's total.

Luckin's principal growth engine remains store expansion. In Q1, the company opened 2,548 net-new stores, or 28 per day (and more than 1 per hour). It ended Q1 with 33.6k global locations, rising 39% y/y.

33.4k of these stores are in China, of which 35% (11.7k) are operated by partners. It's in overseas store openings, however, that the company has set its sights. As of the end of Q1, the company has 177 locations overseas, which breaks down as follows: 82 company-owned stores in Singapore, 12 company-owned stores in the U.S., and 83 franchise-operated stores in Malaysia.
