This brings me to my largest concern surrounding Luckin: with the company's all-encompassing focus on raw store growth, it may be sacrificing efficiency. Luckin is running into a similar problem that Starbucks did in the U.S.: running too many stores and cannibalization. Under new CEO Brian Niccol, Starbucks has been reversing that trend since last fall, closing many underperforming locations in the U.S.

This is clearly evidenced in Luckin's consistent deceleration in same-store sales. In Q1, same-store sales (which measures the revenue growth from existing stores, excluding the contribution from newly opened locations) slid to a -0.1% decline, from 1.3% growth in Q4 and mid-teens growth in Q2/Q3.

But at the same time as the company is investing heavily into new store openings, we note that a greater revenue share is being driven by delivery orders. When pressed on same-store sales in the Q&A portion of the Q1 earnings call, CEO Jinyi Guo acknowledged a very competitive backdrop in delivery orders:

So recently, the delivery war has created very strong growth opportunities in China's coffee consumption. So accordingly, we've been in the phase of a relatively rapid expansion, and this momentum continued into the first quarter. So in this backdrop, some level of fluctuation in our same-store performance is normal. And also, this is well in line with our business expectations. Having said that, same-store sales for our self-operated stores remained broadly stable in the first quarter. We believe it also reflects the resilience of our overall operations."

As a result of the boosted delivery mix, delivery expenses as a percentage of revenue rose to 10.8%, reflecting 380 bps of y/y declines. This coincides with a 340 bps y/y decline in store-level operating margins (excluding corporate overhead costs) to a slim 13.6%, again demonstrating declining efficiency per store:

Also, the hugely promotional and competitive coffee environment has forced Luckin Coffee to step up its marketing and advertising, where nominal expenses rose 48% y/y to RMB 733 million, far outpacing 35% revenue growth and even the 39% y/y growth rate in store count. As a result of lower store operating margins and sharply higher advertising expenses, Luckin's net income declined -3% y/y to RMB 506 million in Q1.

So again, we have to ask ourselves: if Luckin is counting on delivery orders for a greater share of sales (and sacrificing margin to do so), is it optimal for the company to continue building out new stores so aggressively? Its top-line growth is merely an illusion if growth comes at the cost of its bottom line.

Upside Risks, Valuation, And Key Takeaways

In my view, there are more risks than opportunities for Luckin as it exits Q1. That said, we also have to be cognizant of the upside risks that the company enjoys.

We note that Luckin's balance sheet is a major advantage in the company's favor. Luckin has the equivalent of ~$1.30 billion of cash on its balance sheet while having no debt. In other words, the company has quite a long runway and war chest to keep driving unit growth in international markets. Its deep pockets may also allow the company to drive deeper promotions and strategically sacrifice near-term profits to dominate market share in core Chinese markets.

Another potential upside driver for the stock is valuation. Trading at ~8x forward adjusted EBITDA, Luckin is trading far more cheaply than all of its U.S.-based peers, at half the EBITDA multiple of Dutch Bros and Starbucks.

That said, we are very wary of Luckin's negative same-store sales trends and its decline in net income. Sell this stock and invest elsewhere.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Asset Bubbles Cast Long Shadows
data.description:
The Canadian Real Estate Association (CREA) has downgraded its forecast for home sales in 2026, while the number of homes sold across the country in March fell 2.3% from a year earlier.

CREA now expects a total of 474,972 residential properties to be sold in 2026, 1% more than in 2025 and down 30% from the cycle peak of 667,000 home sales in 2021.

In March, the national average sale price fell 0.8% from a year earlier to $673,084, down 18% in nominal terms and about -30% in real terms from the peak of $816,720 in February 2022. Nationally, the nominal Canadian average home sale price is back to its 2021 level. On a real basis, Canadian home prices have retraced 9 years to 2017 levels (shown below, courtesy of CEIC data and the BIS).

The organization of realtors expects the national average sale price to rise 1.5% to $688,955 in 2026 — about $10,000 lower than their January forecast.

On current forecasts, 2022 peak prices are not expected to be recouped in nominal terms until 2029 (WOWA).

After the 1989 bubble peak, home prices in the Greater Toronto Area (GTA) fell 28.5% in nominal terms until 1995. In real (inflation-adjusted) terms, prices did not return to their 1989 highs until 2011 — 22 years later.

China has been leading a global real estate cycle down after its massive property bubble burst in 2021. By 2023, Chinese housing starts had dropped more than 60% from pre-pandemic levels, marking one of the largest housing busts globally in decades.

China’s real residential property price index has seen a record 17 consecutive quarters of decline. In real terms, home values are now where they were in 2006, wiping out 20 years of appreciation for the country’s urban middle class (below via CEIC and the BIS).

There are some striking similarities between fiscal and monetary policy choices made in China and Canada. The following summary should ring some bells for Canadians:

China’s current property crisis stems from the central role of real estate in its economy, which drove rapid growth and contributed about 20% of economic activity. For a decade before the pandemic, housing prices surged relative to household incomes, partly because consumers, facing limited attractive savings options, increasingly invested in property. This fueled heavy borrowing by developers, while local governments became dependent on land sales for revenue.

In 2020, to address concerns about the overheating market, the Chinese authorities introduced the “Three Red Lines” policy aimed at curbing speculative behavior and excessive corporate debt. The policy imposed strict limits on developers’ leverage and liquidity, while banks were restricted from lending to these firms. This credit squeeze then contributed to the collapse of overleveraged developers.

…As property sales plummeted, many developers who relied on pre-sales to finance construction struggled to finish projects. In response, numerous homebuyers launched a “mortgage boycott”, refusing to make payments on unfinished apartments.

This decline was exacerbated by homebuyers’ concerns about developers’ financial health and uncertain property prices, with demand reaching a 13-year low.

In many countries where prices ballooned before the pandemic, home affordability remains untenable for the masses, suggesting prices have further to retrace.

EPB Macro is tracking similar implications for American home prices, employment and the economy, in "The Housing Recession That Never Came — And The One That’s Quietly Underway". Here’s a taste:

“…with the affordability problem in the housing market still extreme, the pace of new home sales will remain sluggish, forcing companies to continue reducing construction activity or carry even more completed inventory.

In either case, the residential construction cycle still hasn’t turned higher. It’s been the same cycle since 2022, just massively drawn out by major construction backlogs and extreme profit margins in the aftermath of the pandemic.

The broader economy will be relatively unimpacted until we see the cycle play out further in residential construction, with material job losses, because it’s the early-stage job losses in sectors like construction that create the problems for the rest of the economy.”

Baby boomers are today aged 63 to 80 and will all be over 65 within 4 years. This cohort owns the largest share of real estate and stock market assets, and they are increasingly looking to downsize holdings to reduce risk, upkeep, and overhead while increasing free cash flow. They need able buyers. Prices lie somewhere between what current owners hope to sell at and what younger cohorts are able and willing to pay.

Disclosure: No positions

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Waste Management Is A Money Printing Compounder
data.description:
In mid-February, I reiterated my buy rating on Waste Management, Inc. (WM) for the second straight time and called the stock a top defensive pick for 2026. I cited margin expansion, robust cash flows, and attractive capital returns as the reasons for my bullish call. You can see in the chart below that there have been modest declines in the stock since then. Meanwhile, the S&P 500 has actually advanced by around 4.6%. With their latest earnings results having come out just yesterday (April 28th), today I'll be providing a fresh analysis to see whether the stock remains a top defensive pick given the current environment.

Below, it is shown that Q1 was a respectable quarter for Waste Management. Revenue growth slowed, but strong expense control meant that margin saw expansion. Furthermore, while the top line underperformed expectations, the bottom line was able to show outperformance. Mixed annual guidance was reaffirmed, but capacity expansion shows that long-term growth is intact. Meanwhile, cash flows continue to be robust, and the stock actually isn't expensive when looking at the P/CFO and P/FCF ratios. Therefore, I have decided to reiterate my buy rating on Waste Management stock.

Growth Slows

Let's get started today with a look at the top-line numbers. For 2026 Q1, Waste Management reported revenues that totaled $6.227 billion, up 3.5% YoY. This is a notable slowdown of growth from 2025 Q4's 7.1% growth, and so overall there has been deterioration in the trajectory of their business momentum. Furthermore, they actually missed analyst expectations by $60 million to show underperformance in the quarter. While this is, of course, disappointing, there are some encouraging signs for investors.

For core price and yield, Waste Management reported 6.3% and 3.9%, respectively. Back in full year 2025, the core price was 6.3% as well, but the yield was at 3.8%. This modest closing of the gap between these two key figures shows that overall the pricing power of the company has improved somewhat.

One of the key reasons why there was growth deceleration was the fact that collection and disposal volumes saw a 1.5% decline. This volume weakness was

primarily due to the impacts of harsh winter weather, intentional shedding of lower-margin residential business, and wildfire cleanup activities that benefitted the prior year period.

In my view, these are reasonable explanations for the softness and indicate that the top-line growth deceleration is less concerning than it seems. The improved yield shows that customers value their services, and that's probably the largest takeaway here.

Expense Controls Are Strong

As a percentage of revenues, adjusted SG&A expenses increased by 10 basis points YoY to 11.1% YoY to show minor inefficiencies in their corporate activities. Despite this, overall expense controls were shown to be strong, as Waste Management was prudent with their operating expenses. As you can see in the table above, adjusted operating expenses as a percentage of revenues dropped by 70 basis points YoY to show that productivity has strengthened. In the earnings release, CEO Jim Fish mentioned "cost optimization" and these numbers clearly indicate that the business is generally well run. Signs of robust profitability also showed up in their margin and bottom line.

The strong discipline with regards to managing operating expenses was clearly reflected in Waste Management's adjusted operating EBITDA margin. As shown above, this key profitability metric expanded from 29.1% in the year-ago quarter to 29.8%. While this improvement is less dramatic than what we saw back in 2025 Q4, margin expansion is almost always good news, and so investors should still be encouraged. Lastly, Waste Management reported an adjusted EPS of $1.81, which represents an 8.4% jump YoY and beat expectations by $0.07. The fact that they missed top-line analyst estimates but beat on the bottom line clearly shows that their profitability is outperforming.

Cash Flows & Capital Returns

In my previous article, I found robust cash flows, and this time around, Waste Management didn't disappoint either. Operating cash flows saw a 24.3% increase YoY. This far outpaces both the top and bottom line growth rates to show that their cash generation ability has been strong. With the phrase 'cash is king' being heard consistently, I believe the numbers here are promising. Furthermore, with CapEx receding from levels seen in the year-ago quarter, FCFs rocketed by a whopping 93.7%. This was clearly a driver of strong capital returns.

While the dividend yield still isn't the highest, I believe investors seeking capital returns should be happy with what they're seeing. Just in early March, the company raised their quarterly dividend by 14.5% to $0.945 per share. As a whole, Waste Management returned almost $730 million to shareholders, which represents the majority of their FCFs seen in the quarter. To be specific, the company paid out $385 million in dividends in Q1, up from $336 million in the prior year period, and bought back $344 million worth of stock versus no repurchases in the year-ago quarter. This shows that the strong cash flows are really paying off handsomely for investors.

Outlook Reaffirmed

After missing on the top line and beating on the bottom line, Waste Management decided to reaffirm their full-year 2026 outlook instead of providing any revisions. While I already talked about these expectations in my previous article, I will go over some highlights again here.

For revenue growth, they project a midpoint of 5.2%, which is a major deceleration from 2025. This was in line with consensus. While reasonable explanations were provided for the softness in Q1, it seems that the trajectory for business activity won't be overly favorable in the near term. For adjusted EBITDA margin, the expectations for a 30 basis point expansion show that prudent expense management will continue. Lastly, for FCFs, they are projecting a midpoint growth of 29.4%, so sizable capital returns should continue.

Despite the slightly mixed near-term guidance, I would say there are signs that long-term growth remains intact for Waste Management. In their Q1, operations reportedly began at their new recycling facilities in Ontario and Detroit. Furthermore, they completed a recycling automation project in South Florida. As a whole, Waste Management states that these projects add almost 300,000 tons of processing capacity, and with recycling seemingly getting ever more important, I would say that the company is well positioned for the long haul.

Valuation Analysis

While Seeking Alpha's valuation page shows that Waste Management is quite expensive according to most multiples, the stock's price tag isn't that high relative to the cash flows being generated. With cash being king and cash flows being an important metric for the company, I believe there are still justified reasons why investors should find the risk/reward to be net attractive right now.

For the P/CFO ratio, the 15.1 reading is a pullback from highs seen earlier this year. Furthermore, Seeking Alpha shows that this multiple is at just a 4.41% premium to the Industrials sector as of this writing. For a premium compounder, that isn't an unreasonable price tag to pay. If you look at their forward P/CFO ratio, Waste Management is actually at a discount to the sector. As for the P/FCF ratio, you can see above that the multiple is very near multiyear lows.

For a slow and steady company like Waste Management, cash flows tell investors a lot about the business' health. While most valuation multiples currently show that the stock isn't cheap, the potentially most important ratios do show that current levels are attractive for entry. Therefore, I continue to view the risk/reward as favorable for the bulls right now.

Conclusion

Waste Management stock hasn't performed as strong as I envisioned back in my previous article, but I believe there are enough reasons for long-term investors to remain bullish. Revenue growth weakness is a risk to be aware of, but it is important to note that the company is intentionally shedding their lower-margin residential business. With expenses getting controlled in a nice manner and cash flows showing robust business health, I believe the company remains well positioned. As discussed above, when looking at the cash flow-based valuation multiples, this premium business actually isn't trading at an overly premium price tag. Therefore, I have decided to reiterate my buy rating on Waste Management stock.
